title: XS650 points ignition timing and condenser failure
description: The XS650 uses a mechanical points ignition system that requires regular adjustment. The contact points wear from mechanical action and the condensers fail from age, causing misfires, hard starting, and eventually no spark. Most XS650s in circulation are 40-55 years old — the original condensers are well past their service life.
causes: Points contact surface wear and pitting
Condenser capacitor failure from age
Timing advance mechanism sticking
Points cam lobe wear
fix_procedure: 1. Check point gap: 0.012-0.016 inches (0.3-0.4mm) with a feeler gauge. 2. Set timing with a timing light: fire at F mark at idle, advance to full by 3000 RPM. 3. If misfiring: replace condensers first ($5 each) — they fail before points. 4. Replace points and condensers as a set every 5K miles or annually. 5. Forum tip: the XS650 points ignition is simple but needs attention every 3K-5K miles. The #1 upgrade is an electronic ignition conversion: Pamco or Boyer Micro Digital ($100-150) eliminates points and condensers entirely. This is the single most impactful reliability upgrade for any XS650. If you're keeping points: always carry spare condensers — they fail without warning. A failed condenser = no spark = dead on the road. The timing advance mechanism (centrifugal weights under the points plate) can stick from corrosion — pull it and clean the pivot points with WD-40 every 10K miles. Stuck advance weights cause flat spots and pinging.
parts_needed: Electronic ignition kit (Pamco or Boyer — recommended upgrade)

title: XS650 charging system — rotor and stator failure
description: The XS650 uses a permanent magnet rotor and stator charging system that was marginal when new and is now failing on every surviving bike. The rotor magnets weaken with age, the stator windings develop shorts, and the voltage regulator (mechanical on early models) can't maintain proper voltage. The result is a bike that runs fine but slowly kills batteries.
causes: Rotor magnet strength degradation from age (40+ years)
Stator winding insulation breakdown
Mechanical voltage regulator contact wear
Brush wear on later models with alternator-style charging
fix_procedure: 1. Test: engine at 3000 RPM, should read 13.5-14.5V across battery. 2. If voltage is low: check stator output first (AC test across leads). 3. If stator output is low: the rotor magnets may have weakened — remagnetize or replace. 4. Upgrade to a solid-state regulator/rectifier (replaces both mechanical units). 5. For custom builds: PMA (Permanent Magnet Alternator) conversion from Hugh's Handbuilt is the gold standard. 6. Forum tip: the XS650 charging system is the weakest link on these 40-50 year old bikes. The original rotor magnets lose 20-30% of their strength over decades — a rotor that's testing borderline today will fail next year. The Hugh's Handbuilt PMA system ($200-300) is the definitive upgrade — it replaces the entire charging system with a modern permanent magnet alternator. For a stock restoration: remagnetize the rotor (specialty service — $50-100) and install a solid-state regulator. The XS650 community has this completely figured out — join XS650.com and search 'charging system upgrade' for detailed how-tos with photos.

title: XS650 oil leaks — engine case, pushrod seals, and gaskets
description: Every running XS650 leaks oil somewhere. The engine cases use paper gaskets that harden and shrink with age, the pushrod tube O-rings deteriorate, and the cam chain tensioner seal weeps. This is a 40-55 year old air-cooled engine with thermal cycling and vibration — leaks are expected and manageable.
causes: Paper gaskets hardened and shrunken from age
Pushrod tube O-ring deterioration
Cam chain tensioner seal failure
Case mating surface corrosion or warping
fix_procedure: 1. Identify the leak source: clean engine thoroughly, ride, then inspect. 2. Pushrod tube O-rings: most common leak, replaceable without splitting cases. 3. Cam chain tensioner seal: accessible from the right side of the engine. 4. Case gaskets: requires engine disassembly. Use quality gaskets (Athena or genuine Yamaha NOS). 5. Forum tip: the XS650 community joke is 'they don't leak oil, they mark their territory.' Every XS650 weeps from somewhere — the question is how much. Pushrod tube O-rings are the easiest fix: $3 for a pair, 30 minutes to replace, and they're the most common leak source. Use Viton O-rings (fuel/heat resistant) — the stock rubber O-rings last 5 years, Viton lasts 15+. For case gaskets, Athena makes the best aftermarket set ($50). Never use RTV silicone on XS650 case surfaces — it squeezes out and can clog oil passages. If the case mating surfaces are corroded, lap them flat on a piece of glass with 400-grit wet sandpaper before reassembly.

title: XS650 cam chain and tensioner — 40+ year old engines
description: The XS650's cam chain stretches over time and the automatic tensioner loses spring tension. The XS650 cam chain runs on the left side of the engine and drives a single overhead cam. A loose cam chain causes timing rattle, and at extreme stretch, can jump timing and bend valves.
causes: Cam chain stretch from 40+ years of use
Tensioner spring fatigue
Chain guide wear
Oil starvation at cold start allows chain slap
fix_procedure: 1. Rattling from the left side of the engine at idle = cam chain. 2. Check tensioner adjustment: the stock manual tensioner has a lock bolt — tighten until rattle stops, then back off 1/4 turn. 3. If the tensioner is at its limit: the chain is stretched beyond adjustment — replace chain and guides. 4. Replacement requires removing the cylinder head and barrels. 5. Forum tip: the XS650 cam chain tensioner is manual on most years (not automatic like modern bikes). This is actually an advantage — you can adjust it precisely and it doesn't have a ratchet mechanism to fail. Check the tensioner at every oil change: loosen the lock bolt, push the tensioner in until resistance, tighten lock bolt. If you're at the tensioner's maximum travel and still getting rattle, the chain needs replacing. Chain replacement is a top-end job ($300-400 at a shop, or a weekend for a home mechanic). Always replace the chain guides at the same time — they're cheap and worn guides accelerate chain wear.

title: RD350/400 oil injection system failure — Autolube
description: The RD350 and RD400 use Yamaha's Autolube oil injection system to deliver two-stroke oil to the engine. The system uses a pump driven by the engine that meters oil based on throttle position and RPM. When the pump, lines, or cable fail, the engine runs without lubrication and seizes — catastrophically and quickly.
causes: Oil pump cable stretch or disconnection
Oil pump seal failure
Oil line blockage or cracking
Oil tank running empty (owner forgot to fill)
fix_procedure: 1. Check oil injection pump cable: should have 0.5-1mm free play. If loose, the pump under-delivers oil. 2. Check oil lines for cracks, kinks, or blockages. Lines harden with age. 3. Verify oil pump operation: with the engine running, disconnect one oil line at the intake manifold — oil should drip steadily. 4. If ANY doubt about the oil injection system: switch to premix immediately. 5. Forum tip: the RD Autolube system works when it works, but a 45-50 year old oil pump is a ticking time bomb. Most serious RD riders delete the Autolube system entirely and run premix at 32:1 (Yamalube 2R or Maxima Super M). This is the safest approach — you KNOW the engine is getting oil because you mixed it yourself. The Autolube deletion takes 30 minutes: remove the pump, block the pump drive, cap the oil lines at the intake, and mix your fuel at 32:1. The oil tank can be converted to an auxiliary fuel tank on the RD400. If you insist on keeping Autolube: rebuild the pump every 5 years and replace all oil lines with new silicone lines.
parts_needed: Premix oil (Yamalube 2R or Maxima Super M)
Oil pump rebuild kit (if keeping Autolube)

title: RD350/400 expansion chamber and exhaust condition
description: The RD's expansion chambers (tuned exhaust pipes) are critical to two-stroke performance — they use reflected pressure waves to improve cylinder charging. Dented, rusted, or incorrect expansion chambers dramatically affect power output. Original RD chambers are rare and valuable; aftermarket options vary widely in quality.
causes: Expansion chamber dents altering tuned length and volume
Internal rust reducing effective volume
Incorrect aftermarket chambers (wrong tuning)
Exhaust gasket leaks at cylinder connection
fix_procedure: 1. Inspect chambers for dents — even small dents change the tuned wave reflection point and affect power. 2. Check for rust holes: hold a light inside and look for pinpoints of light through the metal. 3. Minor dents: carefully reshape with a ball-peen hammer and wooden dolly. 4. If chambers are rusted through: replace. DG, Spec II, or JL are the established aftermarket brands. 5. Check exhaust gaskets at the cylinder — two-stroke exhaust leaks cause loss of crankcase pressure (affects fuel delivery). 6. Forum tip: expansion chambers on a two-stroke are not 'just exhaust pipes' — they're tuned to the engine's port timing and RPM range. The wrong chambers on an RD will make it feel like a completely different (worse) bike. For a stock RD: original Yamaha chambers are the best but cost $500+ for a good pair. For a modified RD: DG pipes are the classic performance choice, Spec II makes excellent replicas of the OEM design. Never use 'universal' two-stroke pipes — they won't be tuned for the RD's port timing. If your chambers have dents, the bike will feel flat at the RPM where the reflection timing is disrupted — you might think the engine is tired when it's actually the exhaust.
parts_needed: Expansion chambers (DG, Spec II, or JL)

title: RD350/400 reed valve inspection and replacement
description: The RD350/400 uses reed valves in the intake to improve low-RPM response and prevent fuel mixture from blowing back through the carburetor. The reed petals are thin steel or carbon fiber that flex open on intake and snap closed on crankcase pressurization. Broken or fatigued reeds cause poor idle, loss of low-end power, and fuel spitting from the carbs.
causes: Reed petal fatigue from age and flexing cycles
Reed petal cracking or chipping
Reed cage warping from heat
Debris lodged preventing full reed closure
fix_procedure: 1. Remove the reed valve assembly (between carb and crankcase). 2. Inspect reed petals: hold up to light — should seal flat against the cage with no light visible through the sealing surface. 3. If petals are chipped, cracked, or warped: replace. 4. Boyesen carbon fiber reed kits are the standard upgrade — better sealing and longer life than stock steel reeds. 5. Check reed cage for warping — replace if the cage surface isn't flat. 6. Forum tip: reed valves are the unsung hero of two-stroke performance. Bad reeds cause symptoms that owners blame on carbs or ignition — rough idle, hesitation off idle, and fuel spitting back through the carb air filter. Reed inspection takes 10 minutes (remove 4 screws) and should be done every 5K miles or whenever the bike runs poorly. Boyesen Power Reeds ($30-40/pair) are a proven upgrade from stock steel reeds — they seal better, open easier at low RPM, and last longer. For a fresh engine build, always install new reeds. The reed cage is often overlooked — if the sealing surface is scratched or warped, new reeds won't seal properly.

title: SR400/500 kickstart-only starting technique and decompression lever
description: The SR400 (and SR500) has no electric starter — it's kickstart only. This is the defining characteristic of the bike and the source of most owner frustration. The single-cylinder thumper has high compression that requires proper technique to start. The decompression lever (on the left handlebar) releases compression for positioning the piston before kicking.
causes: Incorrect kickstart technique
Decompression cable out of adjustment
Weak kick from improper piston positioning
Cold engine requires choke and specific procedure
fix_procedure: 1. The SR400 kickstart procedure: (a) Turn fuel on, choke on. (b) Hold decompression lever, slowly kick past TDC until you feel resistance release. (c) Return kick lever to top. (d) Release decompression lever. (e) Give one strong, full kick. 2. If the kick is weak or the engine doesn't fire: you didn't position the piston correctly. Start over from step (b). 3. If the bike kicks back: compression was not released properly, or timing is off. 4. Check decompression cable adjustment: should have 1-2mm free play. 5. Forum tip: every SR400 owner goes through a learning curve with kickstarting. The key is PISTON POSITIONING — you're using the decompression lever to get the piston just past TDC, then releasing compression so the kick has a full power stroke of compression to work with. If you kick at the wrong point in the cycle, the engine has no momentum to carry through compression. Practice the technique cold: decompression lever in, slow kick to find TDC (you'll feel resistance, then it releases), return to top, release decompression, kick HARD. Within a week you'll start it first kick every time. If the decompression cable is out of adjustment, the lever won't fully release the exhaust valve — and the engine won't have full compression to fire.

title: SR400/500 carburetor and jetting — single Mikuni VM/BS carb
description: The SR400 uses a single Mikuni carburetor (VM type on early models, BS constant-velocity on later models). The single carb is simple but sensitive to jetting — the large single-cylinder engine needs precise fuel metering for clean running. The stock jetting is lean for emissions, causing popping on deceleration and hesitation at part throttle.
causes: Lean stock jetting for emissions compliance
Pilot jet clogging from ethanol fuel
Float level drift from age
Aftermarket exhaust without rejet
fix_procedure: 1. For rough idle and popping: pilot jet is clogged or too lean. Clean pilot jet, consider going up one size (#17.5 to #20). 2. Set float level to spec (check with clear tube method). 3. Remove EPA caps from pilot screw, set to 2 turns out as baseline. 4. For aftermarket exhaust: must rejet — one size up on main and pilot as starting point. 5. Forum tip: the SR400's single carb makes jetting changes easy — one carb, one set of jets. The stock jetting on the 2014+ FI model isn't adjustable, but the carbureted models (all years except 2014+ US) respond beautifully to a mild rejet. Go to #20 pilot (stock #17.5) and #132.5 main (stock #127.5) for a noticeable improvement in throttle response. The SR400 community has decades of jetting data — search 'SR400 jetting' on the SR500 forum for charts specific to your altitude and exhaust setup. For the 2014+ fuel-injected US model: the ECU is sealed and no flash is available. The only fueling modification is a Power Commander, which is overkill for a 400cc single.

title: SR400/500 valve adjustment — screw-type, frequent interval
description: The SR400/500 uses screw-type valve adjusters with a short service interval (every 4000 miles) due to the single-cylinder engine's high per-cylinder load. The air-cooled thumper's wide thermal cycling range accelerates valve seat wear and clearance tightening. Valve adjustment is the most frequent maintenance item on the SR.
causes: Valve clearance tightening from normal single-cylinder wear
Air-cooled thermal cycling accelerating valve seat wear
Deferred maintenance
High compression ratio increases valve train stress
fix_procedure: 1. Check clearance: intake 0.05-0.10mm, exhaust 0.10-0.15mm. 2. The SR has tappet covers on both sides of the head — simple access. 3. Rotate engine to TDC compression (both rockers loose). 4. Adjust with 10mm wrench and screwdriver. 5. Recheck after tightening locknut. 6. Forum tip: the SR400 is the bike that teaches every owner how to adjust valves. The 4000-mile interval sounds frequent but the job takes 10 minutes and the engine is the most accessible of any motorcycle — two tappet covers, two valves, done. The SR community considers valve adjustment a bonding ritual with the bike, not a chore. The big thumper always tightens exhaust first — if you only check one valve, check the exhaust. If the bike is hard to start hot (fires cold, won't restart after riding), the exhaust valve is tight. One turn of the adjusting screw is a huge change on the SR — quarter turns only. Keep a feeler gauge in your tank bag and check at every oil change.
